---
title: "7.2 Artificial Intelligence in GxP Computerized Systems"
subtitle: "3316512 Pharmaceutical AI"
author: Natapol Pornputtapong, PhD
institute: Chulalongkorn University
date: today # today | last-modified
date-format: "DD MMM YYYY" # see https://momentjs.com/docs/#/displaying/format/
pdf-engine: xelatex
format:
    rxquarto-revealjs:
        self-contained: false # set to true to produce a single HTML file
        transition: none # none | fade | slide | convex | concave | zoom
        background-transition: none
        scrollable: false
        code-fold: true
        code-overflow: scroll
        mermaid:
            theme: forest
---
## Introduction to AI in GxP

*   **Technological Advancement:** AI provides new opportunities for the life sciences but introduces complexity and challenges in adoption at scale.
*   **Purpose:** The primary aim is to safeguard **patient safety, product quality, and data integrity** while achieving business benefits.
*   **A Holistic Framework:** The ISPE AI Guide serves as a bridge between GAMP® 5 concepts and the unique characteristics of AI.
*   **Governance:** Safe use is underpinned by organizational structures, policies, and literacy.

---

## AI System Component Hierarchy

The following diagram illustrates how AI models are integrated into a computerized system.

```{dot}
digraph G {
    node [shape=box, fontname="Helvetica", style=filled, fillcolor=lightblue];
    Model [label="Model\n(Sub-program)"];
    Subsystem [label="AI Sub-system\n(Module)"];
    Function [label="AI Function\n(Integration Layer)"];
    System [label="AI System\n(Component)"];
    CompSystem [label="AI-enabled\nComputerized System", fillcolor=orange];

    Model -> Subsystem;
    Subsystem -> Function;
    Function -> System;
    System -> CompSystem;
}
```

---

## The Nine Key Concepts

GAMP® 5 foundations are expanded with four AI-specific building blocks.

| GAMP® 5 Foundations | Additional AI Concepts |
| :--- | :--- |
| Product and Process Understanding | Quality by Design (QbD) |
| Life Cycle Approach within a QMS | Fit for Purpose Data and Data Quality |
| Scalable Life Cycle Activities | Data and Model Governance |
| Science-based QRM | Knowledge Management |
| Leveraging Supplier Involvement | |

---

## Life Cycle Phases

The AI-enabled computerized system life cycle follows four primary phases.

1.  **Concept Phase:** Identification of business needs, solution ideation, and conducting a **Proof of Concept (PoC)**.
2.  **Project Phase:** Planning, model requirement specifications, **iterative experimentation**, and verification/testing.
3.  **Operation Phase:** Performance monitoring, managing **model drift**, and version control through change management.
4.  **Retirement Phase:** Data/model retention, migration, or destruction management.

---

## Iterative Experimentation

At the core of the project phase is the iterative loop to derive the best-performing model.

*   **Model Selection:** Choosing model types and hyperparameters within the defined design space.
*   **Data Engineering:** Pre-processing data into a format suitable for the model.
*   **Model Engineering:** The training or fine-tuning process to establish the model.
*   **Model Evaluation:** Measuring performance against predefined KPIs to guide the next iteration.

---

## Science-Based Quality Risk Management (QRM)

AI specific hazards must be addressed beyond traditional software risks.

*   **Hazard Identification:** Includes data quality shortcomings, inadequate model choice, and model drift during operation.
*   **Functional Risk Assessment:** AI functions are evaluated based on software category, model category, and **AI maturity level**.
*   **Control Strategies:** May include human verification (Human-in-the-Loop), guardrails for Generative AI, and Explainable AI (XAI) methods.

---

## Fit for Purpose Data

A model is only as good as the data used to develop it. Data is "fit for purpose" when it meets four properties:

1.  **Reliability:** Real-world relationships are adequately captured.
2.  **Relevance:** Data is meaningful for the intended use.
3.  **Representativeness:** Data reflects the diversity of real-world situations.
4.  **Abundance:** The amount of data is commensurate with model complexity.

---

## [AI Maturing level in GxP facility ^[https://ispe.org/pharmaceutical-engineering]]{.r-fit-text}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="30%"}
![](pharApp/Picture13.png) ^[https://ispe.org/pharmaceutical-engineering/march-april-2022/ai-maturity-model-gxp-application-foundation-ai]
:::

::: {.column width="70%" style="font-size: 0.5em;"}

| **Stage 1** | **Stage 2** | **Stage 3** | **Stage 4** | **Stage 5** |
|----------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| used in parallel to the normal GxP processes | executing a GxP process automatically but must be actively approved by the operator | executing the process automatically but can be revised by the operator | running automatically and controls itself | running automatically and corrects itself |

| **Stage 1** | **Stage 2** | **Stage 3** | **Stage 4** | **Stage 5** |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| No machine learning | used in a locked state. Updates are performed by manual retraining with new training data sets | Updates are performed after indication by the system with a manual retraining | Updates are performed by automated retraining with a manual verification step | fully automated and learns independently with a quantifiable optimization goal | The system is fully automated and self-determines its task competency and strategy

:::

::::


## Validation levels ^[https://ispe.org/pharmaceutical-engineering]

:::{style="font-size: 0.57em;"}
| Level | Description                                          | Minimum Validation Activities and Requirements                                                                                      |
|-------|------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| I     | Parallel (pharApp/ CS                                     | No validation required                                                                                                              |
| II    | Classical non-AI CS                                  | Validation of computerized system, but no dedicated focus on AI                                                                     |
| III   | Piecewise locked state AI CS                         | Executing the process automatically but can be revised by the operator                                                              |
| IV    | Self-triggered learning AI CS with human operation and update control at all times | In addition to the above requirements: <br> - Documented justification on why a model was selected <br> - Training data verification <br> - Model quality assurance after training <br> - Input data monitoring in operation <br> - Retraining procedures defined |
| V     | Self-triggered learning AI CS with update control, but overall or sampled operation control only | In addition to the above requirements: <br> - Periodic re-test with defined test data set <br> - Assurance of self-control <br> - Control of AI system outcomes by samples for a defined, risk-oriented fraction, and adequate stratification of input/output instances |
| VI    | AI CS with autonomous learning                       | Validation concept currently under development                                                                                       |
:::

## Trustworthy AI Principles

Deploying AI in life sciences requires adherence to human-centric values.

*   **Human Autonomy:** Ensuring humans can effectively interact with and supervise AI.
*   **Transparency:** Communicating benefits, risks, and limitations clearly to users.
*   **Fairness:** Reducing algorithmic bias to ensure equitable outcomes across subpopulations.
*   **Safety & Security:** Minimizing harm and protecting against AI-specific cybersecurity threats like adversarial attacks.

---

## Conclusion & Governance

*   **Accountability:** Regulated companies remain accountable for compliance regardless of supplier involvement.
*   **AI Literacy:** Building foundational understanding across all roles (Process Owners, Data Scientists, Quality Units) is essential for successful adoption.
*   **Continual Improvement:** New data collected during operation should be used to refine and improve AI sub-systems over time.
